stem: A system evolves by \( x_{k+1} = A x_k \), where \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ 0 & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \). Its long-run growth rate is governed by the dominant (largest) eigenvalue of \( A \). What is that dominant eigenvalue?
solution: The matrix is upper triangular, so its eigenvalues are the diagonal entries \( [[a]] \) and \( [[d]] \). The dominant (largest) eigenvalue is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is a diagonal entry, but not the larger of the two eigenvalues.
This is a diagonal entry, but not the larger of the two eigenvalues.
This is the trace (sum of the eigenvalues), not the dominant eigenvalue.
This is the determinant (product of the eigenvalues), not the dominant eigenvalue.
This is one more than the correct dominant eigenvalue.
This is one less than the correct dominant eigenvalue.

stem: Two interacting populations evolve by \( \begin{pmatrix} x_{k+1} \\ y_{k+1} \end{pmatrix} = A \begin{pmatrix} x_k \\ y_k \end{pmatrix} \), where \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & 1 \\ [[s]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \). Since \( A \) is not triangular, solve its characteristic equation to find the growth rate. What is the dominant eigenvalue of \( A \)?
solution: The trace is \( [[a]]+[[d]] = 2([[p]]) \) and the determinant is \( ([[a]])([[d]]) - (1)([[s]]) = [[p]]^{2} - [[t]]^{2} \), so by the quadratic formula the eigenvalues are \( [[p]] \pm [[t]] \). The dominant (larger) one is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the smaller eigenvalue \( [[p]]-[[t]] \), not the dominant one.
This is the \( (1,1) \) entry of \( A \), but \( A \) is not triangular so this is not an eigenvalue.
This is the \( (2,2) \) entry of \( A \), but \( A \) is not triangular so this is not an eigenvalue.
This is half the trace, the average of the two eigenvalues, not the dominant one itself.
This is one more than the correct dominant eigenvalue.
This is two less than the correct dominant eigenvalue.

stem: A sequence \( a_n \) satisfies the linear recurrence \( a_{n+1} = ([[c1]]) a_n + ([[c2]]) a_{n-1} \). In the long run, the ratio \( a_{n+1}/a_n \) approaches the dominant eigenvalue of the associated companion matrix \( M = \begin{bmatrix} [[c1]] & [[c2]] \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \). What is that dominant eigenvalue?
solution: The companion matrix's characteristic polynomial is \( \lambda^2 - ([[c1]])\lambda - ([[c2]]) = (\lambda - [[l1]])(\lambda - [[l2]]) \), so its eigenvalues are exactly \( [[l1]] \) and \( [[l2]] \). The dominant (larger) one is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is one of the two roots, but not necessarily the larger (dominant) one.
This is one of the two roots, but not necessarily the larger (dominant) one.
This is the sum of the two eigenvalues (the trace), not the dominant one itself.
This is the recurrence's second coefficient, not an eigenvalue.
This is one more than the correct dominant eigenvalue.
This is one less than the correct dominant eigenvalue.

stem: A system evolves by \( x_{k+1} = A x_k \), where \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[hi]] & [[b]] \\ 0 & [[lo]] \end{bmatrix} \). Since \( [[hi]] > [[lo]] \), the dominant eigenvalue is \( [[hi]] \), and after \( [[n]] \) steps a generic initial vector is scaled by approximately \( \lambda_{\max}^{[[n]]} \). What is \( \lambda_{\max}^{[[n]]} \)?
solution: The dominant eigenvalue is \( [[hi]] \), and after \( [[n]] \) steps the growth factor is \( \lambda_{\max}^{[[n]]} = ([[hi]])^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the eigenvalue by \( n \) instead of raising it to the \( n \)-th power.
This uses the smaller (non-dominant) eigenvalue instead of the dominant one.
This is off by one in the exponent.
This adds \( n \) to the eigenvalue instead of using it as an exponent.
This is one more than the correct growth factor.
This is three less than the correct growth factor.
